title: Present Simple
description: The foundation of English — used for habits, facts, and routines.
rules: Use for daily routines: "I wake up at 7 AM."
Use for general truths: "The sun rises in the east."
Add -s/-es for he/she/it: "He plays tennis."
Use do/does for questions and negatives.

examples.sentence: She works in a hospital.
examples.explanation: Third person singular — add -s to the verb.

examples.sentence: Do they live in Hanoi?
I don't eat meat.

title: Present Continuous
description: Describe actions happening right now or temporary situations.
rules: Form: am/is/are + verb-ing
Use for actions happening now: "I am studying."
Use for temporary situations: "She is staying with friends."
Some verbs (know, like, want) are NOT used in continuous form.

examples.sentence: They are watching a movie.
Is it raining outside?
He isn't working today.

title: Past Simple
description: Talk about completed actions at a specific time in the past.
rules: Regular verbs: add -ed (worked, played, watched).
Irregular verbs must be memorized (went, saw, bought).
Use with time expressions: yesterday, last week, in 2019.
Questions: Did + subject + base verb?

examples.sentence: We visited Paris last summer.
She didn't come to the party.
Did you enjoy the concert?

title: Present Perfect
description: Connect the past with the present — experiences and recent events.
rules: Form: have/has + past participle
Use for life experiences: "I have visited Japan."
Use for recent actions with present relevance: "She has just arrived."
Time expressions: ever, never, already, yet, just, since, for.

examples.sentence: I have lived here for 5 years.
examples.explanation: Action started in past, continues to present.

examples.sentence: Have you ever tried sushi?
He hasn't finished his homework yet.

title: Conditionals (If clauses)
description: Express hypothetical situations and their consequences.
rules: Zero Conditional: If + present, present — general truths.
First Conditional: If + present, will — real future possibility.
Second Conditional: If + past, would — unreal present/future.
Third Conditional: If + past perfect, would have — unreal past.

examples.sentence: If it rains, I will stay home.
If I were rich, I would travel the world.
If I had studied harder, I would have passed.

title: Future Simple (Will & Going to)
description: Talk about future plans, predictions, and quick decisions.
rules: Will + base verb — predictions and decisions at the moment of speaking.
Going to + base verb — plans or intentions already decided.
Won't = will not for negative future statements.
Time expressions: tomorrow, next month, in two hours, soon.

examples.sentence: I think it will rain this afternoon.
We're going to visit Da Nang next week.
I'll help you with that.

title: Articles (A, An, The)
description: Use articles correctly with nouns in English.
rules: Use "a" before consonant sounds: a book, a university (yoo- sound).
Use "an" before vowel sounds: an apple, an hour (silent h).
Use "the" when the listener knows which thing you mean.
No article with plural general nouns: "Children love games."

examples.sentence: I saw a movie yesterday. The movie was great.
examples.explanation: First mention uses "a"; second mention uses "the".

examples.sentence: She is an honest person.
examples.explanation: "Honest" starts with a vowel sound — use "an".

examples.sentence: The sun rises in the east.

title: Comparatives & Superlatives
rules: Short adjectives: add -er (taller, cheaper) or -est (tallest).
Long adjectives: more/most + adjective (more expensive, most interesting).
Irregular: good → better → best; bad → worse → worst.
Use than in comparisons: "She is taller than me."

examples.sentence: This hotel is cheaper than that one.
It was the most exciting trip of my life.
English is easier than I expected.

title: Modal Verbs
description: Express ability, permission, obligation, and advice.
rules: Can — present ability; Could — polite requests or past ability.
Must / Have to — obligation; Mustn't — prohibition.
Should — advice or recommendation.
Modals are followed by base verb (no to): "She can swim."

examples.sentence: You should see a doctor.
Could you speak more slowly, please?
Students mustn't use phones in the exam.

title: Phrasal Verbs Basics
description: Common verb + particle combinations in everyday English.
rules: Phrasal verb = verb + particle (up, off, on, out) with new meaning.
Some are separable: "Turn off the light" / "Turn the light off."
Some are inseparable: "Look after the baby" (not "Look the baby after").
Context often reveals meaning — learn them in phrases.

examples.sentence: Please turn down the music.
I need to figure out this problem.
She gave up smoking last year.
examples.explanation: "Give up" means stop trying or quit a habit.
